name: Anti-smog Educational Garden
designer: Kraków City Council in cooperation with local environmental experts and educators
description: The Anti-smog Educational Garden is a pilot green infrastructure project located in a school courtyard in Kraków. It combines nature-based elements—smog-absorbing plants, greenhouse, sensory path, compost area, and insect hotels—with an educational program for students. Created to fight high urban air pollution, it promotes health, ecological awareness, and community stewardship through direct interaction with natural systems.
physical_innovation: The project replaces asphalt surfaces with permeable soil and planting zones that use specific species to absorb air pollutants. It integrates a greenhouse, gazebo, benches, rainwater-fed irrigation, and a compost area, creating a multifunctional ecological micro-environment in a dense school courtyard.
social_innovation: The garden is managed by teachers, students, and parents through guided learning activities, composting workshops, and collaborative planting events. It empowers the school community to take collective responsibility for air quality and fosters a culture of environmental care and urban gardening in children from an early age.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

Created to fight high urban air pollution, it promotes health, ecological awareness, and community stewardship through direct interaction with natural systems. | Physical innovation: The project replaces asphalt surfaces with permeable soil and planting zones that use specific species to absorb air pollutants. name: Bosco Verticale (Vertical Forest)
designer: Stefano Boeri Architetti, in collaboration with Arup (engineering) and agronomist Laura Gatti
promoter: Hines Italia, COIMA, Comune di Milano
description: Bosco Verticale (“Vertical Forest”) is a pair of residential towers in the Porta Nuova district of Milan designed to integrate over 20,000 plants, including 800 trees, onto the façades and terraces of high-rise buildings. It combines housing with large-scale urban forestry, acting as a vertical ecosystem that improves air quality, mitigates heat island effects, and promotes biodiversity. The project redefines the relationship between nature and density in high-rise design.
physical_innovation: The project demonstrates how vertical surfaces in dense cities can serve as active ecological systems. The integration of vegetation across the façades and on the rooftops allows for the transformation of building envelopes into living systems that contribute to the urban microclimate and landscape.
social_innovation: Bosco Verticale enhances the liveability of high-rise residential buildings by offering residents access to private outdoor green spaces and views of nature. Although not co-designed, the project spurred public discourse on sustainable living and urban quality, influencing policy and design paradigms.
digital_innovation: The building incorporates sensor systems for plant monitoring (humidity, irrigation), but these are not publicly accessible nor participatory. There is no dedicated digital platform for resident engagement or community interaction linked to the vegetation.

name: Catharijnesingel Canal Re-opening
description: The city of Utrecht has restored its historic ring canal as part of a major redevelopment of the station area. The canal was progressively reopened from 2000 to 2020, restoring almost 6 km of waterway and establishing a green-blue linear park along both banks with interconnected pathways, seating areas, planting, and recreational zones. The project re-established ecological and historical continuity, improved flood resilience, enhanced biodiversity, and prioritized pedestrians and cyclists by reducing motor traffic. It won the European Prize for Urban Public Space in 2022 for its exemplary climate-resilient urban intervention.
physical_innovation: The project replaces a major traffic artery with restored water and adjacent greenery. It introduces stepped canal edges, timber piers, floating platforms, seating docks, and rich planting that connect to the historic Zocherpark. It addresses urban heat and flooding by allowing infiltration, adding canopy shade, and reintroducing the natural water body. The motor traffic was rerouted to prioritize active mobility along the canal.
social_innovation: The canal revival was initiated through a 2002 referendum in which residents voted to remove the highway. The redevelopment has transformed it into a vibrant public space for boating, walking, cycling, and community gathering. It fosters ecological and social inclusivity by reconnecting neighbourhoods and creating shared urban amenities, reinforcing civic heritage and local identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

name: CoolCo’s – Cooling Corners & Corridors
promoter: EIT Urban Mobility, New European Bauhaus initiative, local municipality
description: CoolCo’s Cooling Corners and Corridors are small modular structures made from wood, fabric and steel to contain various plants and flowers. They are designed to reduce the impact of heat waves in urban areas, particularly densely populated neighbourhoods with low heat resilience. Their shape and size can be adjusted to fit strategic locations, sidewalks or walls for instance, where they provide refreshing “islands” of greenery to shelter inhabitants from the heat and improve air quality.
physical_innovation: A set of modular, scalable, and relocatable cooling units designed for rapid deployment in heat-vulnerable urban areas—especially in densely built, low-income neighborhoods. Modular wooden‑steel units combine shading fabrics, plantings, seating, and mist sprayers.Thermal tests during the pilot phase showed a surface temperature drop of 7–19 °C, improving comfort during heatwaves.. The units are anchored with minimal intervention, respecting existing pavements and avoiding underground utility disruption
social_innovation: The project uses a Living Lab co‑design methodology: workshops with elderly citizens, parents of infants, low-income households to place and configure units according to their needs. It empowers vulnerable groups to claim public space, encourages social interaction, and increases awareness of heat resilience with community agency
digital_innovation: Thermal imaging and structured surveys were used during evaluation to collect data on micro‑climate and user perceptions.

name: Corredores Verdes (Green Corridors)
promoter: Municipio de Medellín – Secretaría de Medio Ambiente
description: Corredores Verdes is a large-scale urban reforestation programme that transformed 30 key road corridors and water channels in Medellín into ecological and shaded greenways. The project added over 8,000 trees and 350,000 shrubs, aimed at mitigating the urban heat island effect, improving air quality, enhancing biodiversity, and supporting inclusive mobility. It reclaims public infrastructure—roads, sidewalks, and canals—as green spaces, linking parks, hills, and ecological reserves through continuous vegetated corridors.
physical_innovation: The intervention reconfigures major vehicular infrastructures by narrowing roadways, adding vegetated medians, green embankments, and planted sidewalks. These spaces not only enhance thermal and environmental comfort but serve as structural landscape elements for ecological connectivity across the dense urban fabric.
social_innovation: The project integrated social outreach and inclusion by employing formerly incarcerated people and members of vulnerable communities in planting and maintenance tasks. Public education campaigns and community workshops fostered environmental awareness and shared responsibility for the corridors.
digital_innovation: Although low-tech in its execution, the project applied advanced planning tools including GIS-based climate analysis, ecological connectivity mapping, and microclimate simulation to identify priority zones for intervention and measure environmental impact.

name: Corredor Verde Passeig de Sant Joan
designer: Ajuntament de Barcelona – Urban Ecology Agency, BIMSA, Studio JPAM
promoter: Ajuntament de Barcelona – Urban Ecology Department
description: The Green Corridor of Passeig de Sant Joan is a transformation of a key boulevard into a continuous and multifunctional green urban axis. The intervention reduced vehicle lanes, introduced tree-lined paths, permeable paving, native vegetation, and integrated social spaces. It reinforces ecological connectivity and improves pedestrian comfort and biodiversity in one of Barcelona’s densest areas. It is a model of integrated mobility, greenery, and urban habitability.
physical_innovation: The project physically reorganizes the street section, replacing asphalt with permeable materials, installing two rows of trees, vegetated strips, benches, and native plant beds. It enhances thermal comfort, creates shaded areas, and manages rainwater through soil infiltration. The boulevard becomes a living ecological corridor embedded in the mobility infrastructure.
social_innovation: It supports inclusive public use by creating multifunctional, universally accessible spaces. The project promotes community life through outdoor seating, children's play areas, and shaded social spaces. It also rebalances public space in favor of pedestrians and cyclists, improving urban well-being and quality of life.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

name: Corte Don Bosco
description: An urban regeneration project completed in the San Paolo neighborhood of Bari as part of the G124 program. It serves as a replicable paradigm, a model for regenerating other Italian urban suburbs through strategies of proximity, ecology, and inclusion. The project transformed an anonymous urban void - lacking name, identity, or function - into a recognizable, accessible, and vibrant collective space capable of fostering new social and environmental relationships. The intervention balances low-cost execution with high environmental and social value, and is designed to be replicable in similar underused urban settings.
physical_innovation: The project transforms a previously sealed, heat-intensive urban void into a permeable, climate-adaptive micro-park. The existing impermeable concrete paving was removed and replaced with a 70% permeable surface made of natural binders, inert materials, and quarry stones. 127 young trees were planted during the execution phase; as they mature, they are intended to form a green canopy “roof”, improving thermal comfort through shade and evapotranspiration. At the heart of the intervention is a 24-meter-wide circular clearing (radura), equipped with four circular benches, serving as a social core for collective use and community gatherings.
social_innovation: Through a participatory co-design process, the project connected the technical perspectives of the designers with those of the residents, reinterpreting the natural forms that cross the neighborhood, restoring meaning to a forgotten space. The project emphasizes community engagement from inception, involving residents through meetings and a neighborhood festival. This builds trust and collective ownership. The design promotes local pride, supports public programming, and aims to strengthen social cohesion in a peripheral district lacking identity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

name: Gardens in the Air (Jardines en el Aire)
designer: Nomad Garden in collaboration with local youth, artists, schools, and cultural facilitators
promoter: ICAS (Seville), Ecosistema 41, Nomad Garden, AES Candelaria, with COAS & S+T+ARTS
description: Gardens in the Air is a pilot circular intervention in the Tres Barrios‑Amate neighborhood of Seville (2020–2021). It transforms residential façades by capturing air‑con condensation to feed vertical gardens and uses native aromatic plants to produce community perfume. Co‑design with local youth and public performances activated public space. It won the NEB Prize 2021 under “Buildings renovated in a spirit of circularity”
physical_innovation: The project repurposes discarded AC condensate water to sustain vertical green installations on façade frames. It reclaims building surfaces to create micro-ecological systems using local plantings and perforated containers. The design converts urban infrastructure into living walls contributing visual, ecological, and microclimate transformation
social_innovation: Workshops involved youth (11–16 y/o), neighbors, artists and schools in planting, scent-making and candle-light storytelling events. These activities fostered collective authorship, pride and community ritual — reclaiming public space through sensory, co-creative engagement.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

name: Green Elderly Care, nature & well-being
designer: Multidisciplinary team via Ghent University / local care centre (collaborative design)
promoter: Woonzorgcentrum Het Heiveld / City of Ghent / local schools, community partners
description: Green Elderly Care is a nature-based transformation of a long-term care facility in Ghent, Belgium. It integrates biodiverse gardens, orchards, greenhouses, insect hotels, and animal care areas into the everyday life of residents. Designed with and for elderly people, in collaboration with schools and community members, it fosters well-being, ecological awareness, and intergenerational connection. In 2024, the project was recognised as a finalist of the New European Bauhaus Prizes in the category Reconnecting with Nature, for its innovative integration of social care and green infrastructure.
physical_innovation: Traditional institutional lawns were replaced with biodiverse features: raised vegetable beds, pollinator-rich flower meadows, fruit orchards, beehives, greenhouse, insect hotels and small chicken enclosures. This physical shift embeds therapeutic biodiversity in everyday routines and transforms static spaces into living, dynamic landscapes.
social_innovation: The care centre functions as a community hub where elderly residents, schoolchildren, students and neighbours collaborate in gardening, animal care and seasonal workshops. These activities build intergenerational bonds, promote autonomy among residents, and reshape perceptions of elderly care as a vibrant, communal activity.
digital_innovation: No evidence of embedded digital or smart systems.

name: Green Noise Barrier – Urban GreenUP
designer: Valladolid City Council, CARTIF, University of Valladolid, and landscape consultants
promoter: Urban GreenUP (EU Horizon 2020), Municipality of Valladolid
description: Green Noise Barrier is part of the Urban GreenUP project, a European Horizon 2020 initiative focused on implementing Nature-Based Solutions in cities to mitigate climate change effects, improve air quality, and enhance urban livability. The Green Noise Barrier is a vertical vegetated infrastructure designed to reduce traffic noise and improve environmental quality along a major urban road in Valladolid. Made of modular green panels planted with native species, the barrier acts as a buffer between traffic and adjacent residential zones, improving air quality and aesthetics while providing habitat for urban biodiversity.
physical_innovation: The intervention redefines the roadside edge by installing a continuous vertical barrier of modular panels planted with native vegetation. The green wall system includes structural steel frames anchored to the sidewalk, with integrated planter boxes and automatic irrigation. It replaces bare concrete or fencing with a layered, climate-adaptive interface that reduces noise, enhances pedestrian comfort, and improves ecological continuity along the corridor. Below the barrier, soil beds and micro-greening further soften the transition between hard infrastructure and urban nature.
social_innovation: The project increases perceived environmental quality and contributes to social well-being by improving public space comfort near residential areas. While not co-designed, it supports citizen education about NBS through interpretative signage and public awareness campaigns in surrounding neighbourhoods.
digital_innovation: The project integrates digital environmental monitoring (air quality, temperature, acoustic performance) and remote-controlled irrigation systems.
